report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Jess, thank you for taking the time to share this with us. Your answers paint a really clear picture, and there are some important connections here that we think will reframe how you see what's been going on.

### A Metabolism Running on Empty

The combination you described, energy at 1/10, frequently cold hands and feet, and an inability to lose weight despite real effort, points strongly toward a suppressed metabolic rate. Cold extremities are one of the classic signs that the body isn't producing enough heat and energy at the cellular level, often linked to low active thyroid hormone (T3). Here's the critical piece: the intense exercise programs and likely undereating (low appetite, skipping breakfast, forcing yourself to eat) may actually be deepening the problem. Research shows that [caloric restriction reliably suppresses T3 levels, lowering metabolic rate as an energy-conservation response](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/7061373_Effect_of_Long-Term_Calorie_Restriction_with_Adequate_Protein_and_Micronutrients_on_Thyroid_Hormones), and this suppression can persist even after normal eating resumes. In other words, the very strategies you've used to lose weight may be the reason your body is holding on to it.

### The Breakfast Gap and Hidden Stress

You mentioned low stress, but your biology may tell a different story. Skipping breakfast and running on coffee through the morning is a pattern that [activates the HPA axis and elevates cortisol independently of perceived stress](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3459&context=usdaarsfacpub). Your body reads that morning fasting state as a signal of scarcity and responds with stress hormones to mobilize energy. This can explain the poor sleep as well: when the body relies on stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline to maintain blood sugar overnight (especially with an under-fueled liver), it's common to sleep lightly or wake without feeling restored. The low appetite itself is likely a symptom of this elevated stress physiology, not a sign that your body needs less food.

### Estrogen, Weight Distribution, and Water Retention

The fact that you carry weight specifically at the hips and thighs, combined with puffiness and water retention, fits a recognizable hormonal pattern. [Estrogen promotes subcutaneous fat storage specifically in the hip and thigh region](https://www.infiniskin.com/blog/how-hormones-affect-fat-distribution-and-what-you-can-do-about-it/) through its action on fat cell receptors, and it also drives fluid retention. A suppressed metabolism and elevated stress hormones can impair the body's ability to properly clear estrogen, creating a cycle where the hormonal environment actively resists fat loss no matter how hard you push.

### Everything Connects

What stands out most is how interconnected these patterns are. Undereating suppresses thyroid function. Low thyroid slows metabolism and worsens energy. The body compensates with stress hormones. Stress hormones disrupt sleep, suppress appetite further, and impair estrogen clearance. Estrogen promotes the exact fat storage pattern you're experiencing. Each piece reinforces the others. Your body isn't failing you. It's doing exactly what it's designed to do under these conditions.

---

This quiz gives us a meaningful starting point, but it's still a snapshot. There's more to uncover about the specific drivers behind your patterns and how your body would respond to a different approach. What's clear is that the path forward likely looks very different from what you've already tried.
